summary: Jeff Atwood revisits the once-sacred web design rule about 'the fold' – the idea that critical content must appear before users need to scroll. While the 1996 belief that users don't scroll has been thoroughly debunked, Atwood argues there are still subtle dangers to watch for. He identifies three specific pitfalls: cramming too much above the fold, creating horizontal lines that make pages look complete, and using in-page scrollbars that confuse users. After analyzing his own site's analytics and design, he found they were wasting valuable space at the top with irrelevant UI elements, potentially creating barriers to scrolling for the 25% of users with smaller screens.
key_points: The 1996 rule that 'users don't scroll' was true at the time but became obsolete as users gained web experience – scrolling is now a fundamental skill
Some people still weaponize the outdated fold rule, demanding all content appear above an arbitrary line
Pages that 'look scrollable' encourage exploration – stark horizontal lines that align with the fold are the main factor that stops users from scrolling
Browser chrome (toolbars, tabs, status bars) means the fold is much closer than designers typically assume
The most important content should still go at the top, not because users can't scroll, but because it's basic good design
money_quotes: If you can't learn to scroll within an hour or so of using the web, you're going to have an awfully stunted experience – so much so that you're probably better off not using it at all.
Even today, people will cite the ancient, irrelevant rule of The Fold as if it's still law.
Users will explore and find your content – as long as the page 'looks' scrollable.
The fold is probably much closer than you think it is.
It's not only a basic rule of writing, it's also a basic rule of the web: put the most important content at as close to the top of the page as you can.
The fold is far less important than it used to be, but it isn't quite as mythical as Bigfoot and the Loch Ness Monster quite yet.
key_insight: While the rigid 'above the fold' rule is outdated superstition, thoughtful designers should still prioritize top content and avoid visual elements that accidentally signal a page is complete.
